metadata.summary: Tier-0 router for stack selection. Maps task types to languages and frameworks with format/lint/test commands. Output recommendation with two alternatives and ADR stub.

content.title: Language and Framework Selection Guide
content.summary: A tier-0 decision router for stack selection. Given a project brief, map the task type to the canonical language (Python, TypeScript, Go, Rust) and framework (Django, FastAPI, React, Next.js). Provide format/lint/test commands per language. Output recommendations with two alternatives and an ADR stub — never just a stack name.

why.p: Without an explicit decision record, agents re-litigate stack choice on every session and often pick the framework most represented in recent prompt history, not the best fit. Anchoring to this guide forces a justification artifact (ADR) that future agents and humans can override with evidence.

list.item: Day-zero greenfield: agent must propose a stack for a project brief.
A spec says "use the right tool" — this is the canonical default mapping.
Generating boilerplate format/lint/test commands for CI scaffolding.
Sanity-check: is the chosen language/framework a mismatch for the stated requirements?
Team has an existing stack mandate — don't override without an ADR.
Domains with non-obvious constraints (regulatory, hardware, cloud lock-in) — use a richer ADR flow.
Picking between equally valid options for a short experiment — decision overhead exceeds value.
ML/embedded/blockchain/games — table is not authoritative outside web/API scope.

section.name: language-selection
section.p: Choose Python for backend APIs, data processing, ML pipelines, and scripting. Choose TypeScript for frontend, Node.js backend, and full-stack React apps. Choose Go for high-performance services and CLI tools. Choose Rust for systems programming and WASM.

rule.statement: Choose Python for backend APIs, data processing, ML pipelines, and scripting. Choose TypeScript for frontend, Node.js backend, and full-stack React apps. Choose Go for high-performance services and CLI tools. Choose Rust for systems programming and WASM.
rule.rationale: Each language has a dominant use case where LLM training data coverage and ecosystem maturity give the highest agent productivity. Deviating requires an explicit ADR.

rule.statement: For solo/founder projects, prefer Python (FastAPI or Django) or TypeScript (Next.js) — these have the broadest LLM training-data coverage, meaning agents make fewer mistakes.
rule.rationale: Agent error rate correlates with training data density for the stack. Python and TypeScript dominate training corpora.

section.name: framework-selection
section.p: Use Django for full-featured web apps requiring admin panels, ORM, auth, and migrations. Use FastAPI for modern async APIs and microservices. Use React for complex UIs and SPAs. Use Next.js for full-stack React with SSR/SSG.

rule.statement: Use Django for full-featured web apps requiring admin panels, ORM, auth, and migrations. Use FastAPI for modern async APIs and microservices. Use React for complex UIs and SPAs. Use Next.js for full-stack React with SSR/SSG.
rule.rationale: Framework choice is cheaper to change than language choice. Bias toward the most popular framework in the chosen language unless there is a hard technical mismatch.

section.name: format-and-test-commands
section.p: Modern toolchains have consolidated multiple legacy tools into single, faster binaries.

rule.statement: Use ruff (not black+isort+flake8) for Python formatting and linting. Use biome or prettier+eslint for JavaScript/TypeScript.
rule.rationale: ruff replaces three tools with one, is 10-100x faster, and is actively developed. Agents using the old three-tool setup generate stale boilerplate.

example.title: Modern toolchain commands
example.code:
# Python ruff format src/ && ruff check src/ --fix pytest --cov=src
# JavaScript/TypeScript prettier --write . && eslint . --fix vitest run --coverage
example.note: Modern Python toolchain uses ruff for both format and lint. Tests use pytest with coverage.

example.title: Outdated Python toolchain
example.code: # Outdated Python toolchain black src/ && isort src/ && flake8 src/
example.note: Three separate tools replaced by ruff. Do not generate this pattern for new projects.

section.name: decision-output-requirements
section.p: Stack decisions must be accompanied by explicit justification and alternatives.

rule.statement: When selecting a stack, always output: (1) primary recommendation, (2) two alternatives, (3) ADR stub. Never output only a stack name.
rule.rationale: Forcing two alternatives eliminates "I picked X because X" circular reasoning and documents the decision for future agents and humans.

section.name: adr-stub-template
section.p: Every stack selection for a project living longer than 6 months must produce an ADR with Status, Context, Decision, Alternatives Considered, and Consequences sections. Human sign-off required before implementation starts.

rule.statement: Every stack selection for a project living longer than 6 months must produce an ADR with Status, Context, Decision, Alternatives Considered, and Consequences sections. Human sign-off required before implementation starts.
rule.rationale: Agents are bad at long-tail considerations (compliance, hiring market, vendor lock-in). The ADR forces explicit documentation so the decision can be revisited.

example.title: ADR stub structure
example.code:
# ADR-NNN: Stack selection for <project>
Status: Proposed Date: <YYYY-MM-DD>
## Context <2–3 sentences on requirements>
## Decision <chosen stack>
## Alternatives considered - <alt 1>: rejected because <reason> - <alt 2>: rejected because <reason>
## Consequences - Positive: <bullets> - Negative: <bullets> - Neutral: <bullets>
example.note: The ADR template documents the stack choice for future reference and review.

section.name: agent-gotchas
section.p: Known failure modes when agents select languages and frameworks.

antipattern.description: Agent picks the stack the prompt mentioned most recently, not the one that fits the requirements.
antipattern.reason: Recency bias in LLMs. Randomize the option order in the prompt and anchor the agent to read requirements first.

antipattern.description: Agent recommends last year's defaults (Create React App, webpack, black+isort) instead of current best-practice (Vite, Bun, ruff).
antipattern.reason: Training cutoffs lag. Anchor prompts with the current year and use this methodology as the authoritative current mapping.

antipattern.description: Agent copies format/test commands verbatim into projects with a different directory layout, causing path errors.
antipattern.reason: Commands assume src/ for Python and top-level for JS. Always adapt to the actual project structure before running.

antipattern.description: Agent applies this guide to ML, embedded, blockchain, or game domains.
antipattern.reason: The table covers web/API stacks only. Use domain-specific methodologies for other areas.

section.name: tool-references-and-services
section.p: Modern toolchains and services for language/framework decisions.

list.item: ruff — modern Python toolchain (format + lint): https://docs.astral.sh/ruff
biome — modern JS/TS toolchain (format + lint): https://biomejs.dev
prettier — format JS/TS/JSON/MD where biome is overkill: https://prettier.io
eslint — lint JS/TS: https://eslint.org
gofmt / goimports — format Go (built-in): go.dev
staticcheck / golangci-lint — lint Go: https://golangci-lint.run
cargo fmt / clippy — format + lint Rust: rust-lang.org
pytest / vitest / go test / cargo test — test runners per language
cookiecutter / degit / create-* CLIs — template scaffolders after decision
stackshare.io — reference for "what other companies use for X" — agent can cite
GitHub Trends API — sanity-check on language/framework popularity over last year
Astro / Next.js / Nuxt CLIs — stack-specific scaffolders the agent invokes
Cookiecutter Django / Cookiecutter PyPackage — opinionated templates encoding many defaults
asdf / mise — multi-language version manager — agents pin per project

template.purpose: Architecture Decision Record template for stack selection.
